items.html:
Communication is a human right

Communication is how we connect with others, express who we are, make choices, and stay safe. For the people you support, being understood is not a luxury — it is a fundamental human right. When someone cannot make their needs known, or cannot understand what is happening to them, their dignity, wellbeing, and safety are all put at risk.

  • Dignity: being heard and understood is central to being treated as a person.
  • Choice: people can only make real decisions when information reaches them in a way they understand.
  • Safety: a person who can report pain, worry, or a mistake is far safer.

About this course

This course builds on basic communication skills, but it focuses on supporting people who have additional communication needs. You will learn why communication support matters, how to recognise and reduce barriers, the tools and aids that help, and how to work in a person-centred way.

Please note: this course provides awareness and knowledge only. It does not replace supervised, hands-on training, and it does not replace the assessment and advice of a qualified speech and language therapist (SALT). Always follow the person's care plan, and seek professional guidance for individual needs. If you are ever unsure how best to support someone, ask your manager or the person's speech and language therapist rather than guessing.

Who may have additional communication needs

Many people you support may have communication needs that go beyond everyday conversation. These needs can be lifelong or acquired, permanent or temporary, and they vary enormously from one person to the next. What matters is finding out about the individual in front of you.

  • Lifelong: people with a learning disability or autistic people may communicate differently from birth.
  • Acquired: a stroke, brain injury, or progressive illness can change how someone communicates.
  • Sensory and physical: hearing loss, sight loss, or physical conditions can affect speech and understanding.

Some common examples

A person with a learning disability may need shorter sentences, pictures, or extra time. An autistic person may take language literally and find busy environments overwhelming. Someone living with dementia may struggle to find words or follow a long conversation. After a stroke, a person may have aphasia — difficulty using or understanding language — even though their intelligence is unchanged.

Sensory loss, such as being deaf, hard of hearing, or blind, affects how information is sent and received. Physical conditions affecting the muscles used for speech, such as cerebral palsy or motor neurone disease, can make speech hard to produce. Never assume that difficulty speaking means difficulty thinking.

items.question: Which statement about additional communication needs is correct?

answers.text: Communication needs vary from person to person and can be lifelong or acquired, permanent or temporary.
answers.feedback: Correct. Needs differ for every individual, so you find out what each person needs rather than making assumptions.

answers.text: Only people with a learning disability have additional communication needs.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Additional needs can arise from stroke, dementia, sensory loss, physical conditions, and much more.

answers.text: If a person cannot speak clearly, it means they cannot understand or think clearly.
answers.feedback: No. Many people who find speech difficult understand perfectly well. Never assume difficulty speaking means difficulty thinking.

answers.text: Communication needs are always permanent and never change.
answers.feedback: No. Needs can be temporary or change over time, for example after a stroke or with tiredness or illness.

items.html:
Your role in supporting communication

As a care worker, your role is to support communication and to follow the plan that has been put in place for each person. You are with people day to day, so you are often the first to notice what helps, what frustrates, and what changes over time.

  • Support: use the person's preferred methods, and give them your time and attention.
  • Follow the plan: apply the communication guidance recorded in the care plan consistently.
  • Observe and report: notice and pass on what works and what does not.

The role of the speech and language therapist

A speech and language therapist (SALT) is a qualified professional who assesses a person's communication — and often their eating and swallowing — and advises on how best to support them. They may recommend particular tools, techniques, or aids, and they set out the approach that goes into the care plan.

The two roles work together: the SALT assesses and advises, and you carry out the agreed approach in daily life and feed back what you observe. You should never change a specialist plan on your own — if something is not working, report it so it can be reviewed. If you have any concern about a person's swallowing, seek professional advice promptly.

items.question: As a care worker, it is your job to assess a person's communication needs and decide which specialist tools they should use.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Assessing needs and recommending tools is the role of the speech and language therapist. Your role is to support communication, follow the agreed plan, and report what you observe.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Assessing and advising is the SALT's role. You support communication, follow the plan, and report back what works.

items.html:
Recognising barriers to communication

A communication barrier is anything that gets in the way of a message being sent, received, or understood. Many barriers are created by the environment or by how we communicate — which means we can often remove them.

  • Environmental: background noise, poor lighting, or a busy, distracting space.
  • Physical and sensory: a flat hearing-aid battery, missing glasses, pain, or tiredness.
  • Attitudes and habits: rushing, jargon, talking over people, or assuming someone cannot understand.

Reducing barriers

Small changes make a real difference. Reduce background noise, make sure the room is well lit so your face can be seen, and check that any aids the person uses are working and to hand. Face the person, get down to their level, and give them your full attention.

Watch your own habits too. Speak clearly but naturally, avoid jargon, and do not rush to fill every silence. Above all, presume competence: assume the person has something to say and can understand, and adjust your approach until you find what works. Barriers are usually about the situation, not the person, and recognising this shift in thinking is one of the most useful things you can do. When you look for what is getting in the way, you can nearly always do something about it.

items.question: You need to talk with someone who wears hearing aids, but the television is loud and the room is dim. What is the best approach?

answers.text: Turn the television down, make sure the room is well lit and the hearing aids are working, then face the person and speak clearly.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reducing background noise, improving lighting, and checking the aids all remove barriers so the person can hear and see you.

answers.text: Speak much louder over the television so they can hear you above the noise.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Shouting distorts speech and the noise remains a barrier. Reducing the background noise helps far more.

answers.text: Carry on regardless, as it is not your place to change the environment.
answers.feedback: No. Adjusting the environment to support communication is a core part of your role.

answers.text: Assume they will not understand and speak to their relative instead.
answers.feedback: No. This takes away the person's voice. Presume competence and communicate with the person directly.

items.html:
The total communication approach

Total communication means using every available method, together, to help a person understand and be understood. Instead of relying on speech alone, you combine words with gesture, facial expression, pictures, objects, signing, and writing — whatever helps that individual.

  • Whatever works: there is no single right method; you use the mix that suits the person.
  • Two-way: it supports both how you get information across and how the person responds.
  • Person-led: the person's own preferences guide which methods you use.

Non-verbal communication

Most communication is non-verbal. Facial expression, eye contact, gesture, posture, and tone of voice all carry meaning, and for some people they are the main way they communicate. Pay close attention to a person's body language: it may tell you they are in pain, anxious, happy, or ready to end a conversation.

Be aware of the messages your own body sends. A warm expression, an unhurried manner, and an open posture help a person feel safe and listened to. A glance at the clock or a tense stance can say more than your words. Match your tone and pace to the person, and let them see your face clearly.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the passage about total communication.
items.textField: *Total* communication means using every available method together, combining speech with gesture, pictures, and *signing*. Most communication is *non-verbal*, so a person's body language and facial *expression* often tell you how they feel.

items.html:
Objects of reference

An object of reference is a real object used to stand for an activity, person, or place. Showing someone their coat can mean “we are going out”; a cup can mean “would you like a drink?”. Objects are concrete and easy to understand, which makes them valuable for people who find pictures or words difficult, including some people with a learning disability or a sensory impairment.

  • Consistent: the same object should always mean the same thing.
  • Meaningful: choose objects that connect naturally to the activity.
  • Multi-sensory: an object can be seen, touched, and sometimes heard or smelled.

Giving people time

One of the most powerful things you can offer is time. Many people need longer to process what you have said and to form a response — this is sometimes called processing time. Rushing a person, or answering for them, takes away their voice and can leave them anxious and unheard.

Ask one thing at a time, then pause and wait. Resist the urge to fill the silence or to guess. Give the person space to point, sign, reach for an object, or find their words. A few extra seconds of patience often makes the difference between a person communicating for themselves and being spoken for.

items.question: A person you support takes a long time to respond to questions. What is the most person-centred approach?

answers.text: Ask one question at a time, then pause and give them plenty of time to respond in their own way.
answers.feedback: Correct. Giving processing time and asking one thing at a time lets the person communicate for themselves.

answers.text: Answer for them to save time and keep things moving.
answers.feedback: No. Answering for someone takes away their voice and can leave them unheard.

answers.text: Ask several questions quickly so they only need to respond once.
answers.feedback: No. Multiple questions at once are harder to process. Ask one at a time and wait.

answers.text: Assume no response means they have nothing to say and move on.
answers.feedback: No. A slow response is not no response. Give the person time and watch how they reply.

items.html:
Easy read information

Easy read presents information using simple words and clear pictures side by side, so that written information is easier to understand. Each idea is kept short, the language is plain, and a relevant image sits next to the text to support its meaning. It is widely used to make letters, leaflets, and care information more accessible.

  • Plain words: short sentences and everyday language, one idea at a time.
  • Pictures: images that illustrate and reinforce the meaning of the text.
  • Clear layout: larger text and plenty of space make it easier to follow.

Using easy read well

Easy read is not only a document to hand over — it is a tool to use together. Sit alongside the person, go through it at their pace, and talk about the pictures. This helps you check that the information has genuinely been understood, rather than assuming it has.

Remember that easy read suits many people but not everyone, and it is not a substitute for talking with someone. Some people will prefer photographs of their own life, others symbols, and others will need information spoken or signed. Always match the format to the individual, and offer support to understand it, not just to receive it. A well-made easy-read document can turn a confusing letter into something a person can genuinely take part in, but only if you take the time to read it through with them and answer their questions.

Makaton and signing

Makaton is a language programme that uses signs and symbols alongside speech to support communication. Unlike British Sign Language, which is a full language in its own right used mainly within the Deaf community, Makaton is used together with the spoken word to support understanding, often by people with a learning disability.

  • Signs with speech: you speak and sign the key words at the same time.
  • Symbols: simple line drawings can be used in the same way in writing.
  • Key words: you usually sign only the main words in a sentence.

Picture boards and symbols

Picture boards and symbol charts let a person communicate by pointing to images that represent objects, activities, feelings, or needs. They can be simple, such as a board showing food and drink choices, or more detailed. Symbols give people a reliable way to make choices and express themselves without relying on speech.

To use these tools well, keep them within the person's reach, learn what each symbol means to them, and give them time to point. Consistency matters: everyone in the team should use the same symbols in the same way. If you are supporting someone who uses Makaton or symbols, learn the signs and symbols they rely on so you can meet them halfway.

answers.text: Signs and symbols are used at the same time as speech to support understanding.
answers.feedback: Correct. Makaton uses signs and symbols alongside the spoken word, so speech and signing happen together.

answers.text: It replaces speech completely and is used only within the Deaf community.
answers.feedback: Not quite. That describes British Sign Language more closely. Makaton is used together with speech.

answers.text: It is a written code that only trained therapists can understand.
answers.feedback: No. Makaton is designed to be widely used by families, carers, and people it supports.

answers.text: It is used silently, without any speaking.
answers.feedback: No. With Makaton you speak and sign the key words at the same time.

items.html:
Communication passports

A communication passport is a personal document that describes how someone communicates. It is written with the person and gathers, in one place, the practical knowledge that helps others communicate with them well — especially people who do not know them.

  • How I communicate: the words, signs, sounds, and gestures I use.
  • How to support me: what helps me to understand and to be understood.
  • What my responses mean: how I show yes, no, pain, or distress.

Augmentative and alternative communication (AAC)

Augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) is the term for methods that add to or replace speech. It ranges from low-tech tools, such as communication books and symbol boards, to high-tech AAC devices — electronic aids and apps that speak aloud when the person selects words, symbols, or letters, sometimes controlled by touch or eye gaze.

An AAC device is a person's voice, so treat it with the same respect. Keep it charged, clean, and always within reach, and never move it away or speak for the person. Give people time to build their message, and wait for them to finish. A speech and language therapist sets up AAC; your job is to make sure it is available and to support its use every day.

items.question: It is fine to move a person's AAC device out of the way when you are busy, as long as you give it back later.
items.correctMsg: Correct. An AAC device is the person's voice and must always be within their reach. Moving it away, even briefly, takes away their ability to communicate.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. An AAC device is the person's voice and should always stay within reach, so they can communicate whenever they need to.

items.html:
Hearing aids and glasses

Sometimes the most important communication support is simply making sure a person's everyday aids are working and being used. Hearing aids and glasses are easy to overlook, yet without them a person may seem confused or unresponsive when in fact they simply cannot hear or see clearly.

  • Check daily: make sure hearing aids are switched on and have working batteries.
  • Keep them clean: smeared glasses or a blocked hearing aid reduce their benefit.
  • Encourage use: support the person to wear the aids they rely on.

Interpreters

Some people communicate best in a language other than English, or through British Sign Language (BSL). In these situations a qualified interpreter may be needed, particularly for important conversations such as health appointments or decisions about care. Family members should not be relied on to interpret sensitive or complex information.

When you work with an interpreter, speak directly to the person, not to the interpreter, and use short, clear sentences with pauses. Look at and address the person themselves, keeping your attention on them. The interpreter conveys the words; the relationship and the respect still flow between you and the person you support. Allow extra time, as interpreting takes longer than an ordinary exchange, and check afterwards that the person feels their questions were answered.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the passage about aids and interpreters.
items.textField: Always check that a person's hearing aids and *glasses* are clean and working, as without them someone may seem *confused* when they simply cannot hear or see. For important conversations, a qualified *interpreter* may be needed, and you should speak directly to the *person* rather than to the interpreter.

items.html:
Following the communication care plan

Each person's care plan should include a communication section that sets out how they communicate and how you should support them. This might describe their preferred methods, the aids they use, words or signs that matter to them, and how they show yes, no, or distress. Following it consistently is at the heart of good, safe support.

  • Read it: know the communication plan before and while you support someone.
  • Apply it consistently: everyone in the team should support the person the same way.
  • Keep it current: flag anything that is out of date so it can be updated.

Checking understanding and adapting

Do not assume a person has understood just because they nodded or said yes. Some people say yes to please, or to end a conversation. Check understanding gently — for example by asking the person to show you, or to tell you in their own way what will happen next — rather than asking closed questions that only need a yes.

Be ready to adapt your approach. If one method is not working, try another: add a picture, use an object, slow down, or find a quieter space. Communication can change from day to day, and with tiredness, illness, or mood, so stay flexible and keep watching how the person responds.

items.question: You have explained an activity and the person nods and says yes. What is the best way to check they have understood?

answers.text: Gently ask them to show you, or tell you in their own way, what will happen next.
answers.feedback: Correct. An open check like this confirms real understanding, rather than relying on a nod that may just be agreement to please.

answers.text: Accept the nod as proof they have fully understood.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Some people say yes to please or to end the conversation, so check in an open way.

answers.text: Repeat exactly the same words, only louder.
answers.feedback: No. Volume does not aid understanding. Try a different approach, such as pictures or objects.

answers.text: Move on quickly before they change their mind.
answers.feedback: No. Rushing on risks the person not understanding what is happening to them.

items.html:
The Accessible Information Standard

The Accessible Information Standard applies to organisations that provide NHS or adult social care. It sets out that people who have information or communication needs relating to a disability, impairment, or sensory loss must have those needs met, so they can access services on an equal footing with everyone else.

  • Identify: find out what a person's information and communication needs are.
  • Record: note those needs clearly in their records.
  • Meet and share: act on the needs, and share them, with consent, so others can too.

Working with families and the wider team

Families, friends, and advocates often know a person's communication best, especially when the person cannot easily tell you themselves. With the person's consent, they can help you learn what someone's expressions and sounds mean, and what helps them feel at ease. Value this knowledge while keeping the person themselves at the centre.

Work closely with the speech and language therapist and the rest of the team. Follow the SALT's advice, report how well it is working, and raise any new concerns so plans can be reviewed. Good communication support is a shared effort, and your daily observations are an important part of it.

items.question: What does the Accessible Information Standard require of health and adult social care services?

answers.text: To identify, record, meet, and share people's information and communication needs so they can access care equally.
answers.feedback: Correct. The standard sets out these steps so that disability, impairment, or sensory loss does not prevent equal access to services.

answers.text: To provide all information in easy read to absolutely everyone.
answers.feedback: Not quite. It is about meeting each person's identified needs in the format that suits them, not one format for all.

answers.text: To translate every document into every language automatically.
answers.feedback: No. It is about meeting individual, identified needs, which may or may not involve translation.

answers.text: It applies only to hospitals and not to social care.
answers.feedback: No. It applies across NHS and adult social care, including services like yours.

items.html:
Recording what works

Communication support only stays consistent if what works is written down and shared. When you discover something that helps a person — a sign they respond to, an object that reassures them, or the time of day they communicate best — record it clearly and factually so the whole team can benefit. Without this, useful knowledge stays in one worker's head, and the person has to keep starting again with each new face. Good records mean a person is understood consistently, whoever is on shift.

  • Be specific: describe what you did and how the person responded.
  • Keep it current: update records as the person's needs change.
  • Share it: make sure new information reaches the care plan and the team.

Summary and important note

You have learned why communication is a human right linked to dignity, choice, and safety; who may have additional communication needs; how to recognise barriers and use a total communication approach; the tools and aids available, from easy read and signing to communication passports and AAC; and how to work in a person-centred way. Throughout, remember your role: you support communication and follow the plan, while the speech and language therapist assesses and advises.

Please note: this course has provided awareness and knowledge only. It does not replace supervised, hands-on training, nor the assessment and advice of a qualified speech and language therapist. Always follow each person's care plan, seek professional guidance for individual needs, and speak to your manager about further training.
